The ability to talk through difficult problems you actually solved.</strong></p> <p>The interview question that separates credential-holders from people who can do the work: “Tell me about the most complex problem you have diagnosed and fixed in your homelab or work environment.” If you have a genuinely good answer to that question, the credential conversation is over. If you do not, no stack of certs rescues you.</p> <h2 id="when-a-degree-does-still-matter"> <a href="#when-a-degree-does-still-matter" title="When a Degree Does Still Matter">When a Degree Does Still Matter</a> <a href="#when-a-degree-does-still-matter" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="When a Degree Does Still Matter">#</a> </h2> <p>There are specific contexts where a degree has material value beyond checkbox status:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Federal government and DoD positions</strong>: Many federal IT roles require a degree for specific GS grade levels, and a degree supports security clearance adjudications.</li> <li><strong>FAANG-tier companies</strong>: Large tech companies that filter rigorously early in the process still use degree requirements as screening tools.</li> <li><strong>Moving into management</strong>: Director and VP level IT roles often list degree requirements, and the business credibility a degree provides matters more in executive-adjacent conversations.</li> <li><strong>Graduate programs</strong>: If you want an MBA or an MS in cybersecurity, an undergraduate degree is required.</li> <li><strong>DoD 8140 / 8570 compliance roles</strong>: Academic credentials contribute to role qualification matrices at some clearance levels.</li> </ul> <p>If you are targeting any of these paths, a degree is still worth pursuing. For the vast majority of private sector IT roles at small to mid-size companies, it is one credential among many.</p> <h2 id="the-practical-recommendation"> <a href="#the-practical-recommendation" title="The Practical Recommendation">The Practical Recommendation</a> <a href="#the-practical-recommendation" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Practical Recommendation">#</a> </h2> <p>If you already have a credential (degree or certification), good. Keep adding to your homelab. Document everything. Build the portfolio.</p> <p>If you are considering whether to pursue a credential, ask:</p> <ol> <li>Does the role I am targeting require it as an absolute screen? (If yes, get it.)</li> <li>Will it teach me something I cannot learn faster by building a real lab? (If yes for the right cert, get it.)</li> <li>Is it a multi-year, expensive degree that will not add to my demonstrated skill? (If yes, invest that time and money in homelab hardware, cloud accounts, and a documentation habit instead.)</li> </ol> <p><em>The most employable IT professional in 2026 has the relevant checkboxes, a homelab running in the background 24/7, and a GitHub portfolio that documents months or years of real work. Build all three.</em></p> <h2 id="next-steps"> <a href="#next-steps" title="Next Steps">Next Steps</a> <a href="#next-steps" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Next Steps">#</a> </h2> <ul> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Building an IT Home Lab"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/it-career-playbook/getting-started-in-it/building-an-it-home-lab/"> <a href="/it-career-playbook/getting-started-in-it/building-an-it-home-lab/" rel="follow me" >Building an IT Home Lab</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="CompTIA Certification Roadmap"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/it-career-playbook/it-certifications-and-degrees/comptia-certification-roadmap/"> <a href="/it-career-playbook/it-certifications-and-degrees/comptia-certification-roadmap/" rel="follow me" >CompTIA Certification Roadmap</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="IT Career Playbook Home"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/it-career-playbook-start/"> <a href="/it-career-playbook-start/" rel="follow me" >IT Career Playbook Home</a> </span> </li> </ul> Read More at https://simeononsecurity.com/

item.title: Do I Need a Degree to Become a Software Developer in 2026?
item.category: "do i need a degree to become a developer 2026" , "CS degree 2026 worth it" , "developer without degree 2026" , "coding bootcamp vs degree 2026" , "developer credentials 2026" , "portfolio vs degree developer" , "self taught developer 2026" , "software developer degree requirement AI"
item.description: By mid-2026, a CS degree is an HR filter at some companies, not a differentiator. What actually matters is demonstrated, verifiable, working software. Heres the honest 2026 answer.
item.content:encoded: <h4 id="click-here-to-return-to-the-software-development-career-playbook"> <a href="#click-here-to-return-to-the-software-development-career-playbook" title=" Click Here to Return To the Software Development Career Playbook "> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Click Here to Return To the Software Development Career Playbook"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/software-development-career-playbook-start/"> <a href="/software-development-career-playbook-start/" rel="follow me" >Click Here to Return To the Software Development Career Playbook</a> </span> </a> <a href="#click-here-to-return-to-the-software-development-career-playbook" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title=" Click Here to Return To the Software Development Career Playbook ">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>The honest answer in mid-2026: a CS degree helps you pass some filters, but it does not make you a good developer or get you hired on its own.</strong> What gets you hired is demonstrable proof that you can write and deploy working software, understand the decisions you made, and continue getting better. <em>Degrees and certifications are HR qualifying checkboxes. Your portfolio, your deployed projects, and your interview performance are what actually close offers.</em></p> <p>This is not a dismissal of education. It is an accurate description of what the market rewards in 2026.</p> <h2 id="why-the-credential-signal-has-degraded"> <a href="#why-the-credential-signal-has-degraded" title="Why the Credential Signal Has Degraded">Why the Credential Signal Has Degraded</a> <a href="#why-the-credential-signal-has-degraded" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Why the Credential Signal Has Degraded">#</a> </h2> <p>Five years ago, a CS degree meant a candidate had been through structured coursework in algorithms, data structures, operating systems, and software engineering. That signal was fairly reliable.</p> <p>In 2026:</p> <ul> <li><strong>AI-assisted exam prep</strong> has made it possible to pass certification exams with less hands-on understanding of the underlying concepts.</li> <li><strong>Degree inflation</strong> means nearly every applicant has some form of credential. When everyone has the credential, it ceases to differentiate.</li> <li><strong>Hiring managers at technical teams</strong> have adapted. The first technical conversation now goes much deeper much faster. They know the credential landscape and do not treat a degree as a proxy for demonstrated skill.</li> <li><strong>The volume of AI-generated application materials</strong> means that anything a candidate can fake using AI (resume, cover letter, take-home project completion) carries less weight than things they cannot fake (genuine commit history, deep technical discussion, specific problem stories).</li> </ul> <p>Credentials are still necessary as filters. <strong>CompTIA certs let you pass ATS screens for IT-adjacent roles. A CS degree carries weight at companies that enforce it.</strong> But passing the filter gets you to the interview. The interview is where portfolios and actual skill determine outcomes.</p> <h2 id="the-cs-degree-what-it-is-and-is-not-worth-in-2026"> <a href="#the-cs-degree-what-it-is-and-is-not-worth-in-2026" title="The CS Degree: What It Is and Is Not Worth in 2026">The CS Degree: What It Is and Is Not Worth in 2026</a> <a href="#the-cs-degree-what-it-is-and-is-not-worth-in-2026" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The CS Degree: What It Is and Is Not Worth in 2026">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>What a CS degree is worth:</strong></p> <ul> <li>It passes rigorous edu-credential filters at FAANG and major enterprise employers who enforce it.</li> <li>It provides theoretical depth in algorithms, data structures, systems, and math that genuinely compounds over a long career.</li> <li>It provides access to university recruiting pipelines, internships, and alumni networks that are hard to replicate.</li> <li>It supports security clearance applications and federal employment in certain grade bands.</li> <li>It is still required for many research, ML, and PhD-track roles.</li> </ul> <p><strong>What a CS degree is no longer worth on its own:</strong></p> <ul> <li>A degree without a portfolio of deployed projects does not demonstrate you can build software.</li> <li>A degree without the ability to discuss your codebase at depth does not win technical interviews.</li> <li>A degree without hands-on infrastructure exposure does not substitute for practical DevOps and deployment knowledge.</li> <li>A degree from an institution without an active recruiting presence does not give you the network advantages of attending a well-connected school.</li> </ul> <p><em>The degree is one input, not a complete package.</em></p> <h2 id="bootcamp-vs-self-taught-in-2026-the-portfolio-still-decides"> <a href="#bootcamp-vs-self-taught-in-2026-the-portfolio-still-decides" title="Bootcamp vs Self-Taught in 2026: The Portfolio Still Decides">Bootcamp vs Self-Taught in 2026: The Portfolio Still Decides</a> <a href="#bootcamp-vs-self-taught-in-2026-the-portfolio-still-decides" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Bootcamp vs Self-Taught in 2026: The Portfolio Still Decides">#</a> </h2> <p>Bootcamp graduates and self-taught developers have the same challenge: their credential is less recognized than a CS degree, and they have to compensate by working harder on the thing that actually matters — demonstrable, deployed, documented software.</p> <p>A bootcamp graduate with a strong portfolio consistently outperforms a bootcamp graduate without one, regardless of which bootcamp they attended. The same is true for self-taught developers.</p> <p>What a strong bootcamp graduate brings to a 2026 interview:</p> <ul> <li>Three original projects deployed to real infrastructure with commit history showing development over time.</li> <li>The ability to explain every technology choice under interviewer probing.</li> <li>At least one infrastructure-as-code project showing they understand deployment.</li> <li>A documented incident or bug they diagnosed and fixed.</li> </ul> <p>What a weak bootcamp graduate brings:</p> <ul> <li>A certificate and a GitHub with tutorial clones they cannot discuss at depth.</li> </ul> <p>The outcome of these two interviews is not close.</p> <h2 id="the-practical-framework-for-2026"> <a href="#the-practical-framework-for-2026" title="The Practical Framework for 2026">The Practical Framework for 2026</a> <a href="#the-practical-framework-for-2026" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Practical Framework for 2026">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>If you are deciding whether to pursue a CS degree:</strong></p> <p>Go for it if:</p> <ul> <li>You can complete it without prohibitive debt.</li> <li>You want to eventually work at FAANG, in research, or in highly selective screening environments.</li> <li>You want the alumni network and internship pipeline.</li> <li>You are targeting DoD/federal roles with degree requirements.</li> </ul> <p>Skip or defer it if:</p> <ul> <li>The cost makes the ROI questionable on a developer salary timeline.</li> <li>You can build a portfolio of deployed, documented projects in less time and at lower cost.</li> <li>Your target companies do not enforce the degree requirement (most SaaS, startup, agency, and consultant-hiring companies do not).</li> </ul> <p><strong>In all cases:</strong></p> <p>Start building and deploying software now, regardless of what credential path you are on. The portfolio is not a substitute for credentials at credentialed-filter companies. But it is what decides your career trajectory at every company once you are past the filter.</p> <p>A developer who graduated with a CS degree and immediately started deploying, documenting, and improving real software has the best profile in 2026. A developer who graduated with a CS degree and did nothing outside class work has the same credential as everyone else but nothing more.</p> <p><em>The credential is the floor. The portfolio is the ceiling. Build both.</em></p> <h2 id="next-steps"> <a href="#next-steps" title="Next Steps">Next Steps</a> <a href="#next-steps" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Next Steps">#</a> </h2> <ul> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Building a Developer Portfolio"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/software-development-career-playbook/getting-started-in-software-development/building-a-developer-portfolio/"> <a href="/software-development-career-playbook/getting-started-in-software-development/building-a-developer-portfolio/" rel="follow me" >Building a Developer Portfolio</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Coding Bootcamps vs. Self-Taught vs. Degree"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/software-development-career-playbook/education-and-certifications/coding-bootcamps-vs-self-taught-vs-degree/"> <a href="/software-development-career-playbook/education-and-certifications/coding-bootcamps-vs-self-taught-vs-degree/" rel="follow me" >Coding Bootcamps vs. Self-Taught vs. Degree</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Software Development Career Playbook Home"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/software-development-career-playbook-start/"> <a href="/software-development-career-playbook-start/" rel="follow me" >Software Development Career Playbook Home</a> </span> </li> </ul> Read More at https://simeononsecurity.com/

item.title: Earning Certifications and Advancing in IT: A Promotion Strategy Guide
item.category: "it career advancement" , "it certifications for promotion" , "how to get promoted in it" , "it career growth" , "senior it roles" , "it career strategy" , "moving up in it" , "it career playbook"
item.description: Learn how to use certifications, performance reviews, and strategic career moves to advance your IT career and earn promotions faster.
item.content:encoded: <h4 id="click-here-to-return-to-the-it-career-playbook"> <a href="#click-here-to-return-to-the-it-career-playbook" title=" Click Here to Return To the IT Career Playbook "> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Click Here to Return To the IT Career Playbook"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/it-career-playbook-start/"> <a href="/it-career-playbook-start/" rel="follow me" >Click Here to Return To the IT Career Playbook</a> </span> </a> <a href="#click-here-to-return-to-the-it-career-playbook" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title=" Click Here to Return To the IT Career Playbook ">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>Career advancement in IT does not happen by accident.</strong> Promotions go to professionals who combine technical skill with strategic visibility, documented accomplishments, and the right credentials at the right time. <em>The difference between staying in a role for five years and being promoted in two is usually intentional positioning, not luck.</em></p> <p>IT is one of the few fields where certifications directly influence pay grades and promotion eligibility. Understanding how to use them as career levers is essential.</p> <h2 id="how-certifications-drive-advancement"> <a href="#how-certifications-drive-advancement" title="How Certifications Drive Advancement">How Certifications Drive Advancement</a> <a href="#how-certifications-drive-advancement" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="How Certifications Drive Advancement">#</a> </h2> <p>Most organizations map certifications to job levels. A help desk analyst who earns CompTIA Network+ becomes eligible for sysadmin roles. A sysadmin who earns AWS Solutions Architect opens cloud engineering positions. Certifications signal readiness for the next level without requiring a track record in that role yet.</p> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Certification</th> <th>Career Level It Unlocks</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td><strong>CompTIA A+</strong></td> <td>Help desk, desktop support</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>CompTIA Network+</strong></td> <td>Junior network admin, sysadmin track</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>CompTIA Security+</strong></td> <td>Security analyst tier, DoD 8570 roles</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>AWS / Azure Associate</strong></td> <td>Cloud operations, DevOps pipeline entry</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>AWS / Azure Professional</strong></td> <td>Senior cloud engineer, architect roles</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>CISSP</strong></td> <td>Security manager, CISO track</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>PMP</strong></td> <td>IT project manager, program manager</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>ITIL 4 Foundation</strong></td> <td>IT service manager, operations lead</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p>Earn certifications before you need them. Waiting until a promotion opportunity appears means you are already behind.</p> <h2 id="building-a-visible-track-record"> <a href="#building-a-visible-track-record" title="Building a Visible Track Record">Building a Visible Track Record</a> <a href="#building-a-visible-track-record" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Building a Visible Track Record">#</a> </h2> <p>Certifications open doors. A track record of results closes the offer. Track every accomplishment using the <strong>CAR format</strong>: Challenge, Action, Result. Convert your day-to-day work into measurable outcomes.</p> <ul> <li><strong>Reduced ticket resolution time</strong> from 4 hours to 1.5 hours by building a self-service knowledge base.</li> <li><strong>Migrated 40 servers</strong> to Azure with zero unplanned downtime over a 12-week project.</li> <li><strong>Wrote automation scripts</strong> that eliminated 8 hours of weekly manual reporting.</li> </ul> <p>Bring these numbers to every performance review. Managers advocate for employees who make the advocacy easy.</p> <h2 id="timing-your-promotion-ask"> <a href="#timing-your-promotion-ask" title="Timing Your Promotion Ask">Timing Your Promotion Ask</a> <a href="#timing-your-promotion-ask" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Timing Your Promotion Ask">#</a> </h2> <p>Do not wait for your manager to offer a promotion. You have to request it. The right time is after a major project win, during a performance review cycle, or when you can point to a comp benchmark showing your market rate has outpaced your title.</p> <ul> <li>Research salaries on LinkedIn Salary, levels.fyi, and Glassdoor before the conversation.</li> <li>Prepare a one-page summary: current responsibilities, new responsibilities you have taken on, certifications earned, and accomplishments.</li> <li>Ask for a specific title and salary range, not a vague “raise.”</li> </ul> <p><em>If your company has no clear promotion path, external offers are the fastest way to reset your salary band.</em></p> <h2 id="lateral-moves-as-career-accelerators"> <a href="#lateral-moves-as-career-accelerators" title="Lateral Moves as Career Accelerators">Lateral Moves as Career Accelerators</a> <a href="#lateral-moves-as-career-accelerators" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Lateral Moves as Career Accelerators">#</a> </h2> <p>Sometimes the fastest path up is a step sideways. Moving from desktop support to a cloud team as a junior engineer resets your title clock and puts you on a higher-ceiling track. Lateral moves inside your company cost less than job-hopping but deliver similar trajectory gains.</p> <p>Look for lateral opportunities when:</p> <ul> <li>Your current team has no open senior roles.</li> <li>You have a certification that maps to a different team’s job description.</li> <li>A new project or initiative needs headcount and will give you visible ownership.</li> </ul> <h2 id="mentorship-and-sponsorship"> <a href="#mentorship-and-sponsorship" title="Mentorship and Sponsorship">Mentorship and Sponsorship</a> <a href="#mentorship-and-sponsorship" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Mentorship and Sponsorship">#</a> </h2> <p>A <strong>mentor</strong> advises you. A <strong>sponsor</strong> advocates for you in rooms you are not in. Both matter, but sponsorship is what generates promotions. Build relationships with senior engineers and managers who have seen your work firsthand. Volunteer for high-visibility projects where leadership observes your contributions directly.</p> <h2 id="next-steps"> <a href="#next-steps" title="Next Steps">Next Steps</a> <a href="#next-steps" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Next Steps">#</a> </h2> <ul> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Technical vs. Management Track"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/it-career-playbook/moving-up-in-it/technical-vs-management-track/"> <a href="/it-career-playbook/moving-up-in-it/technical-vs-management-track/" rel="follow me" >Technical vs. Management Track</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="IT Regulations and Compliance"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/it-career-playbook/moving-up-in-it/it-regulations-and-compliance/"> <a href="/it-career-playbook/moving-up-in-it/it-regulations-and-compliance/" rel="follow me" >IT Regulations and Compliance</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="IT Career Playbook Home"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/it-career-playbook-start/"> <a href="/it-career-playbook-start/" rel="follow me" >IT Career Playbook Home</a> </span> </li> </ul> Read More at https://simeononsecurity.com/

item.title: Engineering Management Career Path: From IC to Engineering Manager
item.category: "engineering management career path" , "individual contributor to engineering manager" , "how to become an engineering manager" , "EM vs IC career" , "software engineering manager role" , "engineering manager responsibilities" , "director of engineering" , "managing a development team"
item.description: What the engineering manager role actually involves, how to transition from individual contributor to EM, what the career path looks like beyond EM, and how to decide if management is right for you.
item.content:encoded: <h4 id="click-here-to-return-to-the-software-development-career-playbook"> <a href="#click-here-to-return-to-the-software-development-career-playbook" title=" Click Here to Return To the Software Development Career Playbook "> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Click Here to Return To the Software Development Career Playbook"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/software-development-career-playbook-start/"> <a href="/software-development-career-playbook-start/" rel="follow me" >Click Here to Return To the Software Development Career Playbook</a> </span> </a> <a href="#click-here-to-return-to-the-software-development-career-playbook" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title=" Click Here to Return To the Software Development Career Playbook ">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>The transition from individual contributor to engineering manager is one of the most significant career changes a software developer can make.</strong> The job changes completely. You stop being measured on what you build and start being measured on what your team builds, whether your team is growing, and whether the organization can depend on your team to deliver. <em>This is not a promotion to a better version of the same job. It is a different job that happens to have adjacent roots.</em></p> <h2 id="what-engineering-managers-actually-do"> <a href="#what-engineering-managers-actually-do" title="What Engineering Managers Actually Do">What Engineering Managers Actually Do</a> <a href="#what-engineering-managers-actually-do" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What Engineering Managers Actually Do">#</a> </h2> <p>An engineering manager (EM) owns the people and the process, not the code. Day-to-day responsibilities:</p> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Responsibility</th> <th>What It Involves</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td><strong>1:1 meetings</strong></td> <td>Weekly or bi-weekly individual check-ins with each direct report to discuss growth, blockers, and morale</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Hiring</strong></td> <td>Writing job descriptions, reviewing resumes, running interviews, making offers</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Performance management</strong></td> <td>Setting expectations, delivering feedback, documenting growth and performance risks</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Team planning</strong></td> <td>Sprint planning, roadmap collaboration with PM, capacity planning</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Stakeholder management</strong></td> <td>Communicating team progress, blockers, and risks to product, design, and leadership</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Conflict resolution</strong></td> <td>Addressing interpersonal issues, differing technical opinions, and organizational friction</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Career development</strong></td> <td>Helping engineers identify growth areas and create paths to promotion</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p><strong>What EMs do NOT do in most established teams:</strong></p> <ul> <li>Write production code regularly (occasional PR is fine; daily coding is a red flag for IC neglect).</li> <li>Make unilateral architectural decisions (staff engineers or senior engineers own that).</li> <li>Approve every technical decision (trust your engineers; review the ones with high risk).</li> </ul> <h2 id="is-engineering-management-right-for-you"> <a href="#is-engineering-management-right-for-you" title="Is Engineering Management Right for You?">Is Engineering Management Right for You?</a> <a href="#is-engineering-management-right-for-you" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Is Engineering Management Right for You?">#</a> </h2> <p>Ask yourself honestly:</p> <ul> <li>Do you find more satisfaction in improving someone else’s output than in your own code?</li> <li>Can you have hard conversations (underperformance, behavioral issues, missed promotions) without avoiding them?</li> <li>Are you comfortable having less direct control over technical outcomes?</li> <li>Do meetings and process work energize you, or do they drain you?</li> </ul> <p><em>If the answer to any of these is “that sounds terrible,” stay on the IC track. Both tracks are respected and well-compensated at senior levels.</em></p> <h2 id="transitioning-into-engineering-management"> <a href="#transitioning-into-engineering-management" title="Transitioning Into Engineering Management">Transitioning Into Engineering Management</a> <a href="#transitioning-into-engineering-management" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Transitioning Into Engineering Management">#</a> </h2> <p>The most common path is internal: become a tech lead on your current team, demonstrate people management skills in that role, then transition formally to EM.</p> <p>A staged transition:</p> <ol> <li><strong>Tech lead role</strong>: coordinate technical work for a small team while continuing to code. Observe whether the coordination work energizes or drains you.</li> <li><strong>Interim or acting EM</strong>: step in for a manager on leave or for a team without a manager. Real trial run.</li> <li><strong>Full EM role</strong>: formally transition with explicit scope of responsibility, headcount, and clear success metrics.</li> </ol> <p>External transitions (joining a new company as an EM for the first time) are harder. Most companies want EMs who have managed before, or they require a demonstrated track record of tech lead behavior at minimum.</p> <h2 id="the-career-ladder-beyond-engineering-manager"> <a href="#the-career-ladder-beyond-engineering-manager" title="The Career Ladder Beyond Engineering Manager">The Career Ladder Beyond Engineering Manager</a> <a href="#the-career-ladder-beyond-engineering-manager" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Career Ladder Beyond Engineering Manager">#</a> </h2> <table> <thead> <tr> <th>Level</th> <th>Title</th> <th>Scope</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr> <td><strong>EM</strong></td> <td>Engineering Manager</td> <td>Manages a single team (3–8 engineers typically)</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Sr. EM</strong></td> <td>Senior Engineering Manager</td> <td>Manages a larger team or multiple junior managers</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>Director</strong></td> <td>Director of Engineering</td> <td>Manages multiple teams; owns a domain or product area</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>VP</strong></td> <td>VP of Engineering</td> <td>Manages a department; owns engineering strategy for a product or business unit</td> </tr> <tr> <td><strong>SVP / CTO</strong></td> <td>SVP Engineering or CTO</td> <td>Companywide engineering leadership</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p>Director-and-above roles require demonstrated business acumen, not just people management skill. Budgeting, headcount planning, and board-level communication become part of the job.</p> <h2 id="staying-technical-as-an-em"> <a href="#staying-technical-as-an-em" title="Staying Technical as an EM">Staying Technical as an EM</a> <a href="#staying-technical-as-an-em" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Staying Technical as an EM">#</a> </h2> <p>The best engineering managers maintain technical credibility without blocking their teams’ autonomy.</p> <ul> <li><strong>Read code weekly</strong>: review pull requests on your team. You do not have to write code to understand what is being built.</li> <li><strong>Participate in design reviews</strong>: contribute questions and context, not directives.</li> <li><strong>Stay current in your domain</strong>: follow the major developments in your team’s technical stack.</li> <li><strong>Be honest about your limits</strong>: after 2 to 3 years of management, your hands-on skills will have degraded. Acknowledge this. Lean on your senior engineers for deep technical judgment.</li> </ul> <h2 id="next-steps"> <a href="#next-steps" title="Next Steps">Next Steps</a> <a href="#next-steps" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Next Steps">#</a> </h2> <ul> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Building and Leading a Development Team"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/software-development-career-playbook/managing-a-development-team/building-and-leading-a-development-team/"> <a href="/software-development-career-playbook/managing-a-development-team/building-and-leading-a-development-team/" rel="follow me" >Building and Leading a Development Team</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Software Engineering Technical Tracks"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/software-development-career-playbook/growing-as-a-developer/software-engineering-technical-tracks/"> <a href="/software-development-career-playbook/growing-as-a-developer/software-engineering-technical-tracks/" rel="follow me" >Software Engineering Technical Tracks</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Software Development Career Playbook Home"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/software-development-career-playbook-start/"> <a href="/software-development-career-playbook-start/" rel="follow me" >Software Development Career Playbook Home</a> </span> </li> </ul> Read More at https://simeononsecurity.com/

item.title: Entry-Level IT Jobs in 2026: What AI Changed and What Actually Gets You Hired
item.category: "entry level IT jobs 2026" , "IT jobs AI impact" , "first IT job 2026" , "IT help desk jobs AI" , "IT job search 2026" , "junior IT jobs competitive" , "IT homelab first job" , "getting into IT 2026" , "entry level IT market"
item.description: The entry-level IT job market in 2026 is genuinely harder than it was five years ago. AI automation has reduced headcounts in traditional starting roles. Here is how to differentiate yourself and actually land the first job.
item.content:encoded: <h4 id="click-here-to-return-to-the-it-career-playbook"> <a href="#click-here-to-return-to-the-it-career-playbook" title=" Click Here to Return To the IT Career Playbook "> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Click Here to Return To the IT Career Playbook"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/it-career-playbook-start/"> <a href="/it-career-playbook-start/" rel="follow me" >Click Here to Return To the IT Career Playbook</a> </span> </a> <a href="#click-here-to-return-to-the-it-career-playbook" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title=" Click Here to Return To the IT Career Playbook ">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>The entry-level IT job market in 2026 is the most competitive it has ever been, and AI is the primary reason.</strong> Tier-1 helpdesk, basic network monitoring, and routine system administration tasks — the roles that used to generate reliable entry-level headcount — have been partially automated. Companies that ran 10-seat helpdesk teams in 2022 are running 3-seat teams with AI-assisted ticketing in 2026, and those 3 people are being asked to handle escalations, edge cases, and the work AI cannot resolve. <em>The “get any IT job to get your foot in the door” strategy still works — but the doors are narrower and the competition is much denser.</em></p> <h2 id="what-ai-actually-did-to-entry-level-it"> <a href="#what-ai-actually-did-to-entry-level-it" title="What AI Actually Did to Entry-Level IT">What AI Actually Did to Entry-Level IT</a> <a href="#what-ai-actually-did-to-entry-level-it" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What AI Actually Did to Entry-Level IT">#</a> </h2> <p>Be clear-eyed about this. AI did not eliminate IT jobs. It compressed the entry-level funnel:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Tier 1 helpdesk reduction</strong>: AI-driven self-service portals and chatbots now resolve 40 to 60% of the tickets that used to require a human technician. The humans remaining handle what the AI escalates, which requires more skill, not less.</li> <li><strong>Remote monitoring automation</strong>: AIOps platforms monitor network health, predict failures, and auto-remediate common issues. The NOC analyst role that used to be an entry point is now higher-level.</li> <li><strong>Scripted IT tasks</strong>: routine patching, account provisioning, report generation — tasks that used to justify junior generalist hires — are increasingly automated via Ansible, PowerShell DSC, and enterprise automation platforms.</li> </ul> <p><strong>The result</strong>: fewer purely entry-level roles, higher average expected skill level for the roles that remain, and more competition for each opening.</p> <h2 id="the-real-differentiator-in-2026-can-you-prove-you-can-do-the-work"> <a href="#the-real-differentiator-in-2026-can-you-prove-you-can-do-the-work" title="The Real Differentiator in 2026: Can You Prove You Can Do the Work?">The Real Differentiator in 2026: Can You Prove You Can Do the Work?</a> <a href="#the-real-differentiator-in-2026-can-you-prove-you-can-do-the-work" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Real Differentiator in 2026: Can You Prove You Can Do the Work?">#</a> </h2> <p>Every candidate applying to an entry-level IT role in 2026 has a CompTIA certification or is working toward one. The fraction with a degree has risen. Credentials are table stakes — they get you past the automated resume screen. They do not win you the interview.</p> <p><strong>What actually differentiates candidates now:</strong></p> <ol> <li> <p><strong>A running homelab with documented evidence.</strong> Not “I set up a lab once to study for Security+.” A continuously maintained environment with architecture diagrams, runbooks, an incident log, and a GitHub repository. Interviewers at every level now ask “show me your homelab.” Candidates who can pull up a GitHub repo with diagrams and postmortems in the interview stand out immediately.</p> </li> <li> <p><strong>Genuinely difficult problems you can articulate.</strong> “I diagnosed a VLAN misconfiguration that caused intermittent DNS failures for a specific set of hosts” is a story. “I set up a Windows Server VM” is background noise. Your homelab needs to have generated real problems you had to solve.</p> </li> <li> <p><strong>Demonstrated AI tool proficiency.</strong> Companies are not hiring people to replace AI. They are hiring people who can work alongside and on top of AI tools. Show that you have used Copilot, AI-assisted scripting, or AI-assisted monitoring in your lab work.</p> </li> <li> <p><strong>Documentation skills that prove communication ability.</strong> Nothing demonstrates professional readiness faster than a well-written runbook or incident postmortem. It shows you think in systems, communicate clearly, and consider the next person who has to deal with the same problem.</p> </li> </ol> <h2 id="entry-points-that-still-work-in-2026"> <a href="#entry-points-that-still-work-in-2026" title="Entry Points That Still Work in 2026">Entry Points That Still Work in 2026</a> <a href="#entry-points-that-still-work-in-2026" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Entry Points That Still Work in 2026">#</a> </h2> <p>Despite compression, these entry points remain viable:</p> <p><strong>MSPs (Managed Service Providers)</strong> are the most reliable first-job source. MSPs still need human technicians for client-facing escalations, onboarding, and the long tail of unique client environments. The AI tools help but they do not replace the person who drives to a client site. MSP experience is also extremely valuable: you see dozens of different environments in two years, which compounds your skill base faster than most enterprise roles.</p> <p><strong>Government and defense IT</strong> — federal, state, and DoD IT agencies require real human oversight for compliance, security, and accountability reasons. These are not going away. Clearance-eligible candidates are particularly valuable here, and the pipelines still hire at genuine entry levels.</p> <p><strong>Healthcare IT</strong> — hospital and clinic IT environments have high human service requirements, HIPAA compliance obligations, and legacy system complexity that AI has not touched. Healthcare IT is undersupplied with qualified technicians.</p> <p><strong>Industrial / OT settings</strong> — factories, utilities, and infrastructure operators need IT professionals who understand both IT and operational technology. This crossover is genuinely scarce. If you build OT exposure into your homelab (Raspberry Pi running simulated PLC communications, for example), you are in rare company.</p> <p><strong>Startups and small businesses</strong> — smaller organizations cannot rely on AI tools that require enterprise-level deployment. A 20-person company still needs a real person who can manage their environment. The breadth of responsibility at a small company builds a portfolio faster than narrow specialization at a large one.</p> <h2 id="the-application-strategy-that-works"> <a href="#the-application-strategy-that-works" title="The Application Strategy That Works">The Application Strategy That Works</a> <a href="#the-application-strategy-that-works" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Application Strategy That Works">#</a> </h2> <p>Do not apply randomly. Apply strategically:</p> <ol> <li> <p><strong>Build the homelab before applying.</strong> Your homelab documentation is your portfolio. Without it, you are one of thousands of credentialed candidates. With it, you are one of dozens who can prove they do the work.</p> </li> <li> <p><strong>Apply to MSPs and smaller companies first.</strong> They hire at genuine entry levels and they need people who can do multiple things. Enterprise companies increasingly expect 2+ years of professional IT experience even for “entry-level” posts.</p> </li> <li> <p><strong>Referrals and community.</strong> The majority of IT jobs, including entry-level ones, are filled via referral. Join local IT meetups, Discord servers, and subreddits. Contribute to conversations. Build relationships before you need a job.</p> </li> <li> <p><strong>LinkedIn is your primary professional presence.</strong> Recruiters who fill IT roles search LinkedIn. A complete profile with homelab references, a project section, and relevant skills gets inbound contact. An empty profile does not.</p> </li> <li> <p><strong>Apply to 60 to 80% match postings.</strong> Job descriptions are wish lists. If you meet 65% of stated requirements and can show real hands-on proof, you are worth an interview.</p> </li> </ol> <h2 id="a-realistic-first-6-months-target"> <a href="#a-realistic-first-6-months-target" title="A Realistic First 6 Months Target">A Realistic First 6 Months Target</a> <a href="#a-realistic-first-6-months-target" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="A Realistic First 6 Months Target">#</a> </h2> <p>If you are starting from zero with a certificate but no job:</p> <ul> <li>Month 1: Launch a Proxmox homelab with at least 3 running VMs, an Active Directory domain, and a monitoring stack.</li> <li>Month 2: Document everything. Architecture diagram, service inventory, first runbook. Push it all to GitHub.</li> <li>Month 3: Begin applying to MSPs and SMB IT roles. Use the homelab repo as portfolio evidence.</li> <li>Months 4–6: Iterate on the lab, expand into cloud free tier, document new incidents. Apply persistently. Continue to improve interview storytelling.</li> </ul> <p><em>The people who get stuck for 12+ months find that out after they have been applying without a homelab. The people who build first and apply second move faster.</em></p> <h2 id="next-steps"> <a href="#next-steps" title="Next Steps">Next Steps</a> <a href="#next-steps" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Next Steps">#</a> </h2> <ul> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Building an IT Home Lab"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/it-career-playbook/getting-started-in-it/building-an-it-home-lab/"> <a href="/it-career-playbook/getting-started-in-it/building-an-it-home-lab/" rel="follow me" >Building an IT Home Lab</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="IT Resume Writing Tips"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/it-career-playbook/getting-a-job-in-it/it-resume-writing-tips/"> <a href="/it-career-playbook/getting-a-job-in-it/it-resume-writing-tips/" rel="follow me" >IT Resume Writing Tips</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="IT Career Playbook Home"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/it-career-playbook-start/"> <a href="/it-career-playbook-start/" rel="follow me" >IT Career Playbook Home</a> </span> </li> </ul> Read More at https://simeononsecurity.com/

item.title: Entry-Level Software Developer Jobs in 2026: Why It's Harder and What Works
item.category: "entry level software developer jobs 2026" , "junior developer jobs AI" , "first developer job 2026" , "breaking into software development AI" , "AI junior developer" , "entry level developer competition" , "developer portfolio first job" , "how to get first developer job 2026"
item.description: The entry-level developer job market in 2026 is genuinely harder than 2020–2022. AI has compressed junior hiring. Here is the honest picture and what actually differentiates candidates now.
item.content:encoded: <h4 id="click-here-to-return-to-the-software-development-career-playbook"> <a href="#click-here-to-return-to-the-software-development-career-playbook" title=" Click Here to Return To the Software Development Career Playbook "> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Click Here to Return To the Software Development Career Playbook"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/software-development-career-playbook-start/"> <a href="/software-development-career-playbook-start/" rel="follow me" >Click Here to Return To the Software Development Career Playbook</a> </span> </a> <a href="#click-here-to-return-to-the-software-development-career-playbook" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title=" Click Here to Return To the Software Development Career Playbook ">#</a> </h4> <p><strong>The entry-level developer job market in 2026 is the most competitive it has been since the field professionalized.</strong> AI coding tools have changed the leverage equation: one senior developer with AI tooling does what three juniors did in 2021. Companies have responded by reducing junior headcount while increasing expectations for the junior roles that remain. <em>This is not a reason to give up on a development career. It is a reason to understand what actually gets you hired instead of operating on 2020-era advice.</em></p> <h2 id="the-real-state-of-junior-hiring-in-2026"> <a href="#the-real-state-of-junior-hiring-in-2026" title="The Real State of Junior Hiring in 2026">The Real State of Junior Hiring in 2026</a> <a href="#the-real-state-of-junior-hiring-in-2026" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Real State of Junior Hiring in 2026">#</a> </h2> <p>Be honest with yourself about what changed:</p> <ul> <li><strong>FAANG and large consumer tech companies</strong> cut junior hiring significantly starting in 2023 and have not fully restored it. Their “entry-level” roles now functionally require 1 to 2 years of professional experience or a strong internship record.</li> <li><strong>Mid-market product companies</strong> (Series B and later, 100–500 employees) still hire junior developers but at lower volume than before, and the bar for “entry-level” has risen. They expect a portfolio of deployed projects, not just a degree or bootcamp certificate.</li> <li><strong>Startups, agencies, and smaller companies</strong> are still genuine entry-level hiring environments. They cannot compete on salary with large companies, so they hire earlier-career talent faster and with less credential inflation.</li> <li><strong>Government, defense, and healthcare</strong> continue to hire at genuine entry levels with defined ladders. Clearance-eligible candidates are particularly valued.</li> </ul> <p>The total number of developer job openings is not drastically lower. The number of openings that are accessible without demonstrated experience is lower. And the number of applicants per opening is significantly higher.</p> <h2 id="what-ai-changed-about-the-interview-process"> <a href="#what-ai-changed-about-the-interview-process" title="What AI Changed About the Interview Process">What AI Changed About the Interview Process</a> <a href="#what-ai-changed-about-the-interview-process" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What AI Changed About the Interview Process">#</a> </h2> <p>Hiring managers adapted to AI-assisted applications and AI-assisted code submissions. The response industry-wide has been to increase technical depth in interviews and rely more heavily on portfolio evidence:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Take-home projects</strong>: most companies know that an AI can complete a basic take-home project. Many now use follow-up interviews specifically to probe your design decisions in any code you submitted. If you cannot explain it, it did not come from you.</li> <li><strong>GitHub scrutiny</strong>: recruiters and hiring managers now look at commit history, not just the final state of a repo. A project with a single large commit of clean code looks different from a project with 60 commits showing incremental development. The latter is harder to fake.</li> <li><strong>Portfolio depth</strong>: asking candidates “walk me through a project where something went wrong and how you fixed it” has become more common. This question specifically tests for real experience.</li> </ul> <p><em>The candidate who built three genuinely original projects, deployed them, ran them long enough to hit problems, documented those problems and their solutions, and can explain every architectural decision in a technical conversation — that person stands out in 2026. That is not the average applicant.</em></p> <h2 id="the-portfolio-before-the-application"> <a href="#the-portfolio-before-the-application" title="The Portfolio Before the Application">The Portfolio Before the Application</a> <a href="#the-portfolio-before-the-application" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="The Portfolio Before the Application">#</a> </h2> <p>The single most important thing you can do before applying to developer jobs in 2026 is have a portfolio. Not in the general sense. In the specific sense:</p> <ol> <li><strong>Three real projects</strong> — original work that solves a real problem, not tutorial reproductions. Each deployed and running somewhere.</li> <li><strong>A GitHub repository for each</strong> — with a meaningful commit history showing the project evolved over time, a clear README, and documentation of non-obvious technical decisions.</li> <li><strong>At least one infrastructure project</strong> — a Terraform configuration, a Docker Compose stack with monitoring, or a CI/CD pipeline. This proves you understand software beyond the “write code” step.</li> <li><strong>Something that broke and you fixed</strong> — one documented incident or bug in your work history. This is the interview conversation that separates candidates who did it from candidates who described doing it.</li> </ol> <p>See <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Building a Developer Portfolio"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/software-development-career-playbook/getting-started-in-software-development/building-a-developer-portfolio/"> <a href="/software-development-career-playbook/getting-started-in-software-development/building-a-developer-portfolio/" rel="follow me" >Building a Developer Portfolio</a> </span> for the complete guide.</p> <h2 id="entry-points-that-work-in-2026"> <a href="#entry-points-that-work-in-2026" title="Entry Points That Work in 2026">Entry Points That Work in 2026</a> <a href="#entry-points-that-work-in-2026" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Entry Points That Work in 2026">#</a> </h2> <p><strong>Agencies and software shops</strong> (digital agencies, consulting shops, SaaS development shops) still hire junior developers regularly. They need labor that can execute on client requirements, and they train on the job. The pay is lower than product companies, but the breadth of exposure is enormous — you touch 10 different codebases in your first year.</p> <p><strong>Defense and government contractors</strong> (Booz Allen, SAIC, Leidos, CACI, General Dynamics IT) have consistent hiring pipelines that include genuine entry-level roles, especially for clearance-eligible candidates. Security clearances significantly improve compensation and compete scarcity. If you are clearance-eligible, these opportunities are worth pursuing seriously.</p> <p><strong>Startups at seed to Series A</strong> have the most willingness to take a calculated risk on an early-career candidate with a strong portfolio. The ceiling is lower on immediate compensation, the equity is speculative, but the scope of responsibility is broader and the learning is faster.</p> <p><strong>Embedded/firmware shops</strong> — companies building hardware products (IoT, consumer electronics, medical devices, automotive) remain genuinely starved for developers who understand C/C++ and have any hardware exposure. A Raspberry Pi or Arduino project in your portfolio is meaningful differentiation here.</p> <p><strong>Internal tools and business software companies</strong> — enterprise software, ERPs, logistics software, healthcare software — are not glamorous but have consistent hiring, real product ownership, and stable compensation.</p> <h2 id="what-does-not-work-anymore"> <a href="#what-does-not-work-anymore" title="What Does Not Work Anymore">What Does Not Work Anymore</a> <a href="#what-does-not-work-anymore" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="What Does Not Work Anymore">#</a> </h2> <ul> <li><strong>Applying without a portfolio and expecting credentials to carry you.</strong> Certifications, bootcamps, and degrees are the filter to get your resume read, not the reason to get hired.</li> <li><strong>A GitHub full of tutorial clones and nothing deployed.</strong> Hiring managers see hundreds of these. None stand out.</li> <li><strong>Generic applications to FAANG or top-tier product companies as a first job.</strong> These companies filter aggressively and have raised the bar significantly. Target them as your second or third role, not your first.</li> <li><strong>Applying to job postings without networking.</strong> Referrals move applications ahead of the pile entirely. One referral is worth 50 cold applications.</li> </ul> <h2 id="a-realistic-timeline-for-getting-hired"> <a href="#a-realistic-timeline-for-getting-hired" title="A Realistic Timeline for Getting Hired">A Realistic Timeline for Getting Hired</a> <a href="#a-realistic-timeline-for-getting-hired" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="A Realistic Timeline for Getting Hired">#</a> </h2> <p>If you have a credential but no professional experience:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Months 1–2</strong>: Build your three portfolio projects. Deploy them. Document them.</li> <li><strong>Month 3</strong>: Start community engagement. GitHub, Discord, LinkedIn. Write one technical post about something you built or learned.</li> <li><strong>Month 4</strong>: Begin applying. Target agencies, small companies, startups, and government contractors first.</li> <li><strong>Months 4–8</strong>: Apply persistently while continuing to improve your portfolio. Each interview teaches you something. Iterate.</li> <li><strong>Month 8–12</strong>: If you have not landed yet, the problem is almost always the same — the portfolio is not strong enough or the interview stories are not specific enough. Get honest feedback and fix it.</li> </ul> <p><em>The candidates who take 12 to 18 months to land are almost always spending more time studying or applying and less time building things that prove they can build things.</em></p> <h2 id="next-steps"> <a href="#next-steps" title="Next Steps">Next Steps</a> <a href="#next-steps" class="h-anchor" aria-hidden="true" title="Next Steps">#</a> </h2> <ul> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Building a Developer Portfolio"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/software-development-career-playbook/getting-started-in-software-development/building-a-developer-portfolio/"> <a href="/software-development-career-playbook/getting-started-in-software-development/building-a-developer-portfolio/" rel="follow me" >Building a Developer Portfolio</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Software Developer Interview Preparation"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/software-development-career-playbook/getting-a-job-as-a-developer/software-developer-interview-preparation/"> <a href="/software-development-career-playbook/getting-a-job-as-a-developer/software-developer-interview-preparation/" rel="follow me" >Software Developer Interview Preparation</a> </span> </li> <li> <span itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/WebPage"> <meta itemprop="name" content="Software Development Career Playbook Home"> <meta itemprop="url" content="/software-development-career-playbook-start/"> <a href="/software-development-career-playbook-start/" rel="follow me" >Software Development Career Playbook Home</a> </span> </li> </ul> Read More at https://simeononsecurity.com/
